content.text: When a third-party AI vendor — Anthropic or a system integrator — experiences a security incident, service disruption, or data breach, the firm must execute its own incident response in parallel with the vendor's. The firm cannot delegate its regulatory obligations to its vendor: GDPR Article 33 requires the firm to notify its supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, regardless of whether the breach occurred at a vendor.

This sub-subtopic covers the firm-side incident coordination playbook for Claude-related vendor incidents: how to declare, how to coordinate with the vendor, how to assess the impact on firm data, and how to meet notification obligations.
Vendor incidents affecting Claude deployments fall into three categories:
Anthropic API logs accessed without authorisation; firm prompt data visible in vendor breach
Regulatory notification obligation; data subject rights
Anthropic API returning 5xx errors for >15 minutes; inference endpoint unreachable
Business continuity; SLA breach; downstream decision delays
Model version deprecated without notice; output characteristics shift materially
MRM validation invalidated; decision quality degraded
Anthropic publishes real-time API status. Monitor programmatically (the status page exposes a JSON endpoint) and alert when any component drops below 'Operational'.
The health monitor from the previous sub-subtopic surfaces degradation before it appears on the vendor status page.
Anthropic will send incident notifications to the contact address in the DPA. Ensure this routes to a monitored security operations inbox, not an individual's email.
Production users encountering unexpected errors or changed outputs may be the first signal of a capability change incident.
The firm's incident commander — not the vendor — controls the firm's response. Assign the incident commander role from a pre-designated rota; do not wait for the vendor to declare first.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path
import json

class IncidentSeverity(str, Enum):
    P1 = "P1"   # Critical: data breach confirmed or likely; full API outage >30 min
    P2 = "P2"   # High: API degraded >15 min; capability change confirmed
    P3 = "P3"   # Medium: intermittent errors; capability change suspected
    P4 = "P4"   # Low: advisory only; no confirmed firm impact

class IncidentStatus(str, Enum):
    DECLARED = "declared"
    INVESTIGATING = "investigating"
    CONTAINED = "contained"
    RESOLVED = "resolved"
    CLOSED = "closed"

@dataclass
class VendorIncident:
    incident_id: str                  # e.g. "INC-2026-031"
    vendor: str                       # "anthropic" | "rag_platform" | ...
    description: str
    detected_utc: str                 # ISO timestamp
    declared_utc: str                 # when firm declared the incident
    severity: IncidentSeverity
    status: IncidentStatus
    incident_commander: str
    vendor_ticket_id: str = ""        # vendor's own ticket reference
    affected_models: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    affected_workloads: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    personal_data_involved: bool = False
    dpo_notified: bool = False
    regulator_notified: bool = False
    regulator_notification_deadline: str = ""
    timeline: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    def log_event(self, description: str, actor: str) -> None:
        self.timeline.append({
            "ts_utc": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            "actor": actor,
            "event": description,
        })

    def save(self, base_dir: str = "evidence/incidents") -> None:
        path = Path(base_dir) / f"{self.incident_id}.json"
        path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        import dataclasses
        path.write_text(json.dumps(dataclasses.asdict(self), indent=2))

# Declare a P1 data incident
incident = VendorIncident(
    incident_id="INC-2026-031",
    vendor="anthropic",
    description="Anthropic notified us of unauthorised access to inference logs affecting API customers. Assessing whether firm prompt data was included.",
    detected_utc="2026-06-20T09:15:00Z",
    declared_utc="2026-06-20T09:30:00Z",
    severity=IncidentSeverity.P1,
    status=IncidentStatus.DECLARED,
    incident_commander="Sarah Chen, CISO",
    affected_models=["claude-opus-4-8-20251101"],
    affected_workloads=["MDL-2026-047 Credit Memo Assistant", "MDL-2026-052 Fraud Narrative"],
    personal_data_involved=True,   # assumed until confirmed otherwise
    regulator_notification_deadline="2026-06-23T09:15:00Z",  # T+72h from detection
)
incident.log_event("Incident declared. Vendor contacted. DPO alerted.", "Sarah Chen")
incident.save()

The firm's response team must interface with the vendor's incident team while simultaneously running its own investigation. These two workstreams must be kept distinct — do not assume the vendor's assessment of impact is complete or accurate for the firm's specific data.
Identify the vendor's security incident contact from the DPA or MSA. This should be pre-populated in the firm's runbook — do not search for it during the incident.

Open a formal support ticket with the vendor referencing the firm's DPA and requesting: (a) the nature and scope of the incident, (b) whether firm data (prompts, completions, metadata) was involved, (c) the timeline of the breach, and (d) the vendor's containment and remediation actions.
Request hourly status updates during the acute phase. The vendor's public status page is insufficient — it typically shows service availability, not data exposure scope.
Designate a single point of contact on the firm side to receive all vendor communications. Route copies to the incident commander, DPO, and Legal.
Document every vendor communication with a timestamp. In a regulatory inquiry, the record of what the vendor told the firm and when is essential for demonstrating good-faith response.

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import json

def assess_data_exposure(
    incident_start_utc: str,
    incident_end_utc: str,
    affected_models: list[str],
    prompt_log_dir: str = "evidence/api_traces",
) -> dict:
    """
    Independently assess which firm prompts were sent to the affected vendor
    during the incident window, to determine personal data exposure.
    This does NOT rely on vendor disclosure — it analyses firm-side logs.
    """
    start = datetime.fromisoformat(incident_start_utc.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    end = datetime.fromisoformat(incident_end_utc.replace("Z", "+00:00"))

    log_dir = Path(prompt_log_dir)
    exposed_calls = []
    total_scanned = 0

    for log_file in log_dir.glob("*.json"):
        try:
            record = json.loads(log_file.read_text())
        except Exception:
            continue
        total_scanned += 1
        ts_str = record.get("timestamp_utc", "")
        if not ts_str:
            continue
        ts = datetime.fromisoformat(ts_str.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
        model = record.get("model_id", "")
        if start <= ts <= end and model in affected_models:
            exposed_calls.append({
                "log_file": log_file.name,
                "ts_utc": ts_str,
                "control_id": record.get("control_id", "UNKNOWN"),
                "output_tokens": record.get("result", {}).get("output_tokens"),
            })

    return {
        "incident_window": {"start": incident_start_utc, "end": incident_end_utc},
        "total_logs_scanned": total_scanned,
        "calls_in_window": len(exposed_calls),
        "personal_data_assessment": "REQUIRES_MANUAL_REVIEW" if exposed_calls else "NO_CALLS_IN_WINDOW",
        "exposed_call_sample": exposed_calls[:10],  # first 10 for manual review
    }

If the independent assessment confirms (or cannot rule out) that personal data was involved, the notification clock is running. The firm — as data controller — bears the notification obligation; the vendor's own regulator notification does not satisfy this.
Lead supervisory authority (e.g., ICO in UK)
Unencrypted personal information exposed to unauthorised party
System outage or breach affecting banking operations

Do not delay the 72-hour GDPR notification waiting for the vendor to confirm the scope of the breach. If you cannot rule out personal data exposure, notify and update the notification as new information becomes available. Regulators penalise delayed notification more heavily than an initially incomplete one.

from datetime import datetime, timezone

def draft_gdpr_notification(
    incident: dict,
    firm_name: str,
    dpo_name: str,
    dpo_email: str,
) -> str:
    """Draft a GDPR Article 33 supervisory authority notification."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M UTC")
    return f"""GDPR Article 33 Personal Data Breach Notification
Submitted: {now}
Submitting Organisation: {firm_name}
Data Protection Officer: {dpo_name} ({dpo_email})

1. NATURE OF THE BREACH
{incident['description']}

2. CATEGORIES AND APPROXIMATE NUMBER OF INDIVIDUALS CONCERNED
Categories: {incident.get('data_categories', 'Under investigation')}
Approximate number: {incident.get('individual_count', 'Under investigation — estimate pending log analysis')}

3. LIKELY CONSEQUENCES
{incident.get('consequences', 'Risk of unauthorised access to prompt data submitted to the Anthropic API. Personal data potentially included in prompts is subject to that access.')}

4. MEASURES TAKEN OR PROPOSED
Immediate: API access suspended for affected workloads pending scope confirmation.
Short-term: Independent log analysis to determine which prompts were in the exposure window.
Vendor: Anthropic contacted; awaiting confirmation of scope and containment timeline.
Regulator: This notification submitted within 72-hour deadline. Further update to follow once scope is confirmed.

5. CONTACT FOR FURTHER INFORMATION
{dpo_name}, DPO — {dpo_email}
"""

# Usage
notification = draft_gdpr_notification(
    incident={
        "description": "Unauthorised access to Anthropic API inference logs during 2026-06-20 09:00–11:00 UTC.",
        "data_categories": "Potentially: names, financial identifiers, account numbers included in credit memo prompts.",
        "individual_count": "Up to 340 individuals referenced in prompts during the exposure window (estimate).",
        "consequences": "Risk that third party accessed prompt content containing personal financial data.",
    },
    firm_name="Acme Financial Services Ltd",
    dpo_name="Jane Smith",
    dpo_email="dpo@acmefinancial.com",
)
print(notification)
After containment, the firm must conduct a structured post-incident review and update its TPRM controls:
Root cause analysis: determine whether the incident was preventable with stronger contractual requirements, different data minimisation practices, or different vendor choice.
Control gap assessment: identify which monitoring controls failed to detect the incident early and which escalation steps were delayed.
TPRM record update: record the incident in the vendor's TPRM assessment file and factor it into the next formal re-assessment score.
Contract review: assess whether the incident constitutes a contractual breach and whether the right-to-audit clause should be exercised.
Board and committee reporting: include a post-incident summary in the next TPRM committee report, covering timeline, regulatory obligations met, and lessons learned.
Playbook update: revise the incident runbook to address any gaps surfaced during the response.

Vendor incident coordination for Claude deployments requires the firm to run its own parallel response — independent of the vendor's investigation — because regulatory obligations (GDPR 72-hour notification, OCC 36-hour rule) fall on the firm as data controller. The firm-side response covers incident declaration, vendor communication, independent impact assessment from firm-side logs, regulatory notification drafting, and post-incident control updates. Pre-populated runbooks with vendor contact details and regulatory notification templates reduce time-to-action during an active incident.
